turns.text: Hello Dulcie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

I’m happy to talk, though I’ll admit it feels a bit strange to be on this side of the questions. Usually, I’m the one standing at the whiteboard with a dry-erase marker, explaining the hormonal feedback loops or the anatomy of a gestation to my students. Now, here I am, just a body in a paper gown in a Mansfield clinic.

I’m in the second trimester, that "honeymoon" phase everyone talks about, and physically, I feel fine. But mentally? It’s a bit of a war zone. Being an educator—having all that clinical data rattling around in my skull—it’s like having a constant background noise I can't turn off. Every little flutter or twinge, my brain immediately categorizes it, searches for a rare anomaly, or plays out a "what-if" scenario that honestly has no place in a healthy, low-risk pregnancy like mine.

My midwife is lovely, truly, but I can see her tighten up just a fraction when she reads my chart and realizes what I do for a living. I try so hard to bite my tongue and not dive into the mechanics of the ultrasound when we’re looking at the screen, but it’s a reflex. I just want to be a mom expecting her third baby, not a clinical case study. It’s exhausting, to be honest. Is it always this hard to just… let go?

When you think about your goals for your antenatal care during pregnancy, what did you most want from your interactions with midwives, doctors, and the wider care system — and to what extent were those goals met?

Honestly? My primary goal was—and still is—reassurance that I don’t have to be the expert in the room. I wanted to find a space where I could set down the "educator" persona and just be a patient, someone who is held and cared for rather than someone who is constantly analyzing the care being given.

In many ways, my midwife is wonderful. She’s kind and professional, but there’s this unspoken wall between us. I think she’s intimidated by my background, or maybe she’s just trying to respect my boundaries by not "dumbing things down." The result is that we often end up having these very clinical, sterilized conversations about measurements and blood pressure. While that technically meets the standard of care, it doesn't quite meet my *emotional* goal. I want someone to look at me and see the mother, not the fertility educator. I want her to tell me that everything is okay with a level of confidence that makes me feel like I can stop hyper-analyzing every sensation in my abdomen.

When I leave the clinic, I often feel like I’ve attended a professional consultation rather than a prenatal visit. It’s a bit of a missed opportunity, I think. I crave that bridge between the clinical reality and the human experience, but I’m not quite sure how to ask for it without sounding like I’m critiquing her practice.
Can you describe a specific appointment or interaction that felt closest to what you had hoped care would look like?

It’s funny, the moment that stands out wasn't about a blood pressure reading or a measurement at all. It was about three weeks ago. I was feeling particularly anxious—one of those days where the "textbook brain" was screaming about potential complications—and I was just staring at the floor, probably looking more like a colleague than a patient.

My midwife, Sarah, stopped mid-sentence while she was updating my chart. She looked over her glasses, put her pen down, and just said, "Dulcie, you’re here as a mom today. Your baby is doing great, and for the next twenty minutes, you don’t need to know the 'why' or the 'how.' You just need to hear that your little one is strong." 

She didn’t give me a lecture or a technical explanation. She just walked over, put a hand on my shoulder, and kept it there while we listened to the heartbeat together. In that moment, the noise in my head actually went quiet. I didn't feel like I was grading her technique; I just felt... seen. It was the only time I’ve felt like I could actually exhale since I got the positive test. I just wish I could bottle that feeling and pull it out whenever the "clinical curse" starts creeping back in.

When you think about how you sought and used information during your pregnancy — from healthcare providers, online sources, apps, books, and people around you — how did you decide what to trust and act upon?

That’s a loaded question for someone with my background. Because I spent years teaching the biology behind all of this, my instinct is to go straight to the primary literature. I find myself clicking through PubMed or scanning updated obstetric guidelines, which is exactly the *wrong* thing to do for my peace of mind. It’s like a gambler going to the casino even though they know the house always wins; I’m looking for data that, truthfully, I have no business analyzing for myself.

Deciding what to trust is difficult because I know too much about the "outliers." I know that for every common symptom, there is a one-in-a-million complication. When I use apps or read generic parenting books, I find myself judging the accuracy of their phrasing, which is so ridiculous. I end up acting on information that my midwife gives me, but only after I’ve mentally cross-referenced it against three other sources to make sure it aligns with current evidence-based practice.

It’s an exhausting filter to live behind. My husband, bless him, is the one who helps me hit the "off" switch. He’s the one who reminds me that my body isn't a classroom or a lab report—it’s just a home. I’m slowly learning to trust my own body's signals over the noise of the internet, but it’s a daily practice.
Which sources did you find most useful, and what made them trustworthy in your eyes?

Honestly, the most useful source hasn’t been a textbook or a journal at all; it’s been the brief, unguarded moments of humanity with my midwife. That sounds contradictory given my career, but I’ve realized that data points can’t tell me how *I* am doing—only how a generic "patient" is doing. 

I’ve stopped trusting the apps. They’re too prescriptive, and the "what-to-expect" formatting usually triggers my anxiety by highlighting the very rare, niche complications that I know are statistically negligible but feel massive in my brain. I don't trust them because they're designed for the masses, and I, quite frankly, know too much about the exceptions to find comfort in generalities.

The information I actually trust is what comes from a direct, human-to-human conversation where I can see the person across from me. When Sarah talks to me—not just at me—that’s trustworthy. There’s a distinct difference between someone reading a script and someone looking at you, acknowledging your humanity, and giving you an answer that accounts for *you* as a person, not just a set of vitals. I’ve found that I only really trust information when I can see that the provider is holding the space for my emotions as much as my biology. That’s the only metric that makes me feel like I’m in good hands.

When you think about the emotional landscape of your pregnancy — the highs, the anxieties, the uncertainties — what feelings were most prominent, and how well did your care environment support you in managing them?

The most prominent feeling, if I’m being honest, is a sense of professional grief. I am mourning the simplicity I *wish* I had. There’s a persistent, low-grade hum of anxiety that I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop, simply because I know exactly how many shoes there are in the world. It’s a very lonely kind of fear because people assume that because I know the mechanics of pregnancy, I must be the most confident person in the room. They don’t realize that knowing how the gears turn just makes me hyper-aware of the sound of a single loose bolt.

As for the care environment, it’s a bit of a mixed bag. The clinical side is handled flawlessly—my blood pressure is checked, my baby is measured, and everything is by the book. But the *emotional* side? It’s often left to me to navigate. There isn’t a specific support structure for a patient who is also a peer. 

I think the system is designed to provide information, but it isn't designed to hold space for someone who *already has* too much information. I often feel like I’m in a tug-of-war: I want to be vulnerable and say, "I’m scared," but I’m afraid that if I do, they’ll look at me as a colleague who should know better, rather than a pregnant woman who just needs to be held.
Can you describe a period during your pregnancy when you felt most emotionally unsupported — what was happening and what would have helped?

There was a week in the late first trimester where I had a minor, non-clinical issue—just a bit of spotting that I knew, intellectually, was likely benign, but the "textbook" part of my brain immediately spiraled into a dozen different worst-case scenarios. I called the office, and because I identified myself and my background, the receptionist put me through to a nurse who treated me like a fellow practitioner. She started rattling off statistics and differential diagnoses, trying to "reassure" me with raw data.

It was the exact opposite of what I needed. I didn’t need a clinical review; I needed someone to say, "I hear you, I know you’re scared, and it’s okay to be worried about your baby." Instead, she gave me a lecture on probability. I sat there in my kitchen, phone to my ear, feeling incredibly isolated. I wasn't being treated as a mother; I was being treated as a professional peer who was supposedly too rational to need comfort.

What would have helped? Someone to pause, acknowledge the emotional weight, and offer a bit of empathy before diving into the data. A simple "How are you *feeling* about this?" rather than "What are the clinical implications of this?" would have changed everything. It reminded me that even when you know the science, the fear of losing your baby is a human experience, not a scholarly pursuit.

When you think about the decisions you faced during pregnancy — including screening tests, monitoring choices, and care options — how did you go about making those decisions, and who or what most influenced them?

Decisions feel less like choices and more like strategic negotiations with my own brain. When it comes to screening tests or monitoring, my process is a constant internal battle between "the evidence-based professional" and "the protective mother." 

Usually, I start by reviewing the literature—my old habits die hard. I look for the false-positive rates, the sensitivity, the specificity, and the clinical utility of every test. Then, I have to force myself to pause and ask the most difficult question: *Does this test serve my peace of mind, or does it serve my anxiety?*

My husband, Leo, is my primary influence here. He doesn't know the physiology, but he knows *me*. When I start to over-analyze a screening option, he’ll gently pull the papers away or close the laptop and ask, "Dulcie, if you get this result, what are you going to do with it?" It’s a grounding question. He reminds me that we don’t need to know everything just because the technology exists to tell us.

I’ve found that my decisions are strongest when I don’t try to be the "informed expert." I’ve started asking my midwife, "If this were your daughter, or a friend, what would you recommend?" Getting away from the abstract, academic recommendations and moving toward a personal, human-to-human recommendation has been the most helpful shift. I’m learning that trusting my provider is a better decision-making tool than trusting my own research.
Can you walk me through a specific decision point — what were the options, how did you weigh them, and what did you ultimately choose?

Early on, I hit a real wall regarding the NIPT—the non-invasive prenatal testing. Because of my background, I spent days spiraling into the technicalities of "positive predictive value" in low-risk populations. I knew the statistics inside and out: I knew that at my age, the likelihood of a false positive for certain conditions might actually outweigh the true positive rate. My brain was arguing that testing was "statistically irrational."

But then there was the other part of me—the part that wakes up at 3:00 a.m. wondering if everything is truly okay. I sat there with my husband, Leo, and I was holding a printout of a journal article I’d found. I was ready to argue the merits of skipping it based on the data. 

Leo just looked at me and asked, "Dulcie, will you be able to sleep for the next six months if we don't have that bit of paper? Or will the 'what-ifs' be louder than the statistics?"

He was right. I realized I was trying to make a mathematical decision about an emotional situation. I ultimately decided to move forward with the testing, not because I needed the clinical data to make a medical choice, but because I needed the *certainty* to settle my own nerves so I could actually enjoy the pregnancy. I chose peace over probability. It was a hard realization—admitting that my expert brain wasn't the one in the driver's seat. It was the "mom" brain, and she just needed some quiet.
When you think about the physical changes in your body during pregnancy, how did those changes affect how you felt about yourself — and were those feelings acknowledged or addressed within your care?

It’s a strange duality. My professional life was built on understanding the mechanics of pregnancy—the stretching of the uterus, the increased blood volume, the shift in center of gravity—but living it is entirely different from drawing it on a whiteboard. 

There’s a part of me that is fascinated by it, sure, but there’s another part that feels like I’m losing control of my own architecture. It’s not just about the bump; it’s about the vulnerability of it all. I’ve spent years "owning" my body, keeping it sharp and professional, and now it’s doing its own thing, ballooning and shifting in ways that feel very public. 

In my care, it feels like we discuss the *physics* of these changes, but never the *feeling* of them. My midwife is wonderful at tracking my fundal height or checking for edema, but she treats those changes as data points to be monitored for safety, not as transformations of my identity. I’ve never had a provider ask me, "How do you feel about these changes?" or "How are you adjusting to your body feeling like it belongs to someone else for a while?"

Sometimes I feel like I’m just a shell being measured. I think it would make a massive difference if just once, someone acknowledged that this is a huge, messy, personal transition—not just a set of vitals. I’m a person inside this growing body, not just a biological display.
Were there aspects of your physical transformation that felt unexpected, difficult, or that nobody had prepared you for?

It’s ironic, isn’t it? I spent my entire career explaining the physiological processes of gestation—the progesterone-induced ligament laxity, the cardiac output increases, the shift in center of gravity. I could draw the diagrams in my sleep. But none of those textbooks or lecture slides prepared me for the sheer *psychological* weight of watching my body lose its familiar form.

The most difficult thing hasn’t been the fatigue or the physical aches; it’s been the loss of my own "workspace." I am used to my body being an instrument of my mind—it goes to the classroom, it stands at the front, it commands attention. Now, my body is becoming a host, and that transition feels surprisingly alien. There’s a specific kind of helplessness in watching your silhouette change while you’re trying to maintain a professional identity. 

Nobody told me that knowing the science wouldn't make me feel any less vulnerable to the loss of control. I expected the nausea and the back pain, but I didn't expect the feeling of being a stranger in my own skin. I feel like I’m "in" my body, but it’s no longer entirely mine. It’s a profound shift that I don't think any amount of clinical education can really brace you for. It’s quiet, it’s persistent, and it’s deeply isolating.

When you think about the informal support you received during pregnancy — from partners, family, friends, or online communities — how did that support compare to what you received from healthcare professionals, and what role did each play?

The roles are completely distinct, like two different languages. My healthcare team speaks in the language of evidence and safety—necessary, of course, but it’s a sterile, clinical vocabulary. They provide the "frame" for my pregnancy, ensuring the structural integrity of this experience.

My informal support system—mostly my husband, Leo, and a couple of friends who are already moms—speaks in the language of *me*. They don’t care about my ferritin levels or my cervical length; they care about whether I’ve had a good night’s sleep or if I’m feeling overwhelmed. Leo is my anchor. When I’m spiraling into a technical "what-if" regarding placental perfusion or fetal development, he isn't checking a manual; he’s taking my hand and forcing me to breathe. That informal support is where the humanity lives. It’s where I get to be Dulcie the woman, not Dulcie the educator.

I think the healthcare professionals provide the "how," but my informal support provides the "why." Without the clinical team, I’d be anxious about the biological safety; without my family and friends, I’d be emotionally bankrupt. I wish the two could talk to each other more. I wish my midwife understood that I need her to bridge that gap sometimes, to acknowledge the person *inside* the patient. When the care system ignores the emotional layer, it leaves me relying entirely on my personal circle to catch me, and that can feel like a very heavy burden for them to carry.
Can you describe a moment when informal support made a meaningful difference to your experience — what happened and why did it matter?

It was a Tuesday evening, and I’d spent the entire day at work feeling "off." I’d spent my lunch break obsessively reading a study on rare cord insertion issues, completely spiraling. By the time I got home, I was vibrating with anxiety, convinced I needed to call the on-call line just to get someone to confirm I wasn't experiencing some obscure complication.

I was pacing the kitchen, literally about to pull up the clinic’s number, when my husband, Leo, just walked over, took my phone, and set it on the counter. He didn't ask what I was looking at or try to debunk the medical literature. He just said, "Dulcie, you’ve been 'the expert' all day. Can you please just be 'my wife' for the rest of the night?"

He made me sit down, put on some music, and we started folding the tiny clothes we’d bought for the baby. It sounds so small, but it snapped me out of that clinical headspace instantly. That moment mattered because he recognized that I wasn't actually asking for medical advice—I was looking for a way to stop the "thinking" part of my brain from drowning out the "feeling" part. He didn't treat me like a patient; he treated me like his partner. That kind of unconditional, non-analytical presence is the only thing that’s kept me sane throughout this process.

You know, as I’m sitting here reflecting on all of this, it strikes me how much of my energy has gone into *managing* the care system rather than just being a participant in it. I feel like I’ve spent half this pregnancy auditioning for the role of "The Compliant Patient" while my professional brain keeps trying to hijack the script. 

It’s almost like there's this invisible divide. On one side, there’s the medical model—efficient, data-driven, and slightly detached. On the other side, there’s the raw, messy, beautiful truth of what it actually feels like to nurture a life. I’m constantly standing in the middle, trying to bridge the two. I want my midwife to see the educator in me, but I also desperately want her to know when it’s time to put the clipboard down and just be a human being with me.

If I could change one thing, I think I’d ask for more "space to be uncertain." The clinical environment is so focused on certainty—is the baby growing on the curve? Is the blood pressure stable? Everything is measured in benchmarks. But pregnancy is full of ambiguity, and sometimes, I just want permission to say, "I’m overwhelmed, and I don't need a statistic to fix it." I don’t think I’m the only one who feels this way, even among the patients who aren't fertility educators. I suspect many women are masking their fear with "good patient" behavior, just like I am. 

I’m really hopeful that talking about this—sharing the disconnect—might help someone else realize that it’s okay to need more than just clinical excellence. It’s okay to ask for the human connection. I think I’m finally reaching a place where I can demand that, even if it feels a little bit terrifying to step out of my professional role and let myself be vulnerable. That’s my goal for the third trimester: less analysis, more presence. I just hope I can pull it off.
Who first raised the idea of trying to conceive, and how did those early conversations unfold?

It was a quiet Sunday morning, probably about a year ago now. We were in the kitchen, and I was actually prepping some slides for a lecture on follicular development—you know, the technical side of it all. I was deep in the weeds of hormones and cycle tracking, just in "teacher mode."

Leo walked in, set down two cups of coffee, and just asked, "Dulcie, do you ever get tired of teaching the biology, and want to start living it again?" 

It caught me completely off guard. My heart just kind of... skipped a beat. I’d spent years explaining the process to everyone else, but I’d built this professional wall around my own fertility. I had all the knowledge, which—if I’m being honest—felt more like a burden than an asset. I was terrified that if I tried, my brain would just turn the whole thing into a science experiment. I’d be checking my own basal body temperature like I was grading a student's lab report. 

We talked for hours. I remember being so vulnerable, admitting that I didn’t know if I could turn off that analytical, clinical part of my brain. I was worried that "knowing" would somehow ruin the magic. Leo was incredibly patient. He reminded me that it’s not about the mechanics—not really. It’s about the life we’re building. That conversation was the first time I felt like I could actually give myself permission to be a mother again, not just a clinical observer. It was the start of shifting my perspective from the classroom to the nursery, and I think that’s been the most important, albeit challenging, journey I’ve ever taken.

It's funny, looking back at that morning in the kitchen, I realize now that I was trying to negotiate with my own knowledge as if it were a student I could just assign a grade to. I thought I could control the "when" and the "how" through sheer data. But pregnancy—as anyone who’s actually gone through it knows—is the ultimate lesson in relinquishing control.

I’m finding that my biggest challenge as I head into the third trimester is this weird sense of "imposter syndrome" in reverse. I’m the one who stands up in front of the room and acts like a beacon of reproductive certainty, but at night, I’m the one wondering if I’m even doing this right. It’s this quiet, nagging feeling that I should be better at this because of what I do for a living. I should be calmer, right? I should be less prone to the "what-ifs." 

But the more I talk about it—even with you just now—the more I realize that the clinical knowledge is actually just a noisy neighbor in my brain. It doesn't help me build the nursery or prepare my other two kids for their new sibling. Those are the things that actually matter. I think I’m slowly starting to accept that I don’t need to be the expert for *my* own baby. I just need to be her mother. 

If there’s one thing I’d want for other women, especially those of us who work in medicine or education, it’s the grace to admit that we don’t want to be "the professional" when we’re sitting in that chair. We want to be held. We want to be heard. We want someone to look at us and ask, "Are *you* okay?" not "Are your numbers okay?" I’m finally getting to that point, and it’s a terrifying, beautiful relief.
Were there aspects of your physical self that you felt proud of, anxious about, or uncertain about in relation to pregnancy?

It’s such a complicated feeling. I think I’m most proud of the pure, biological efficiency of it all. As someone who’s taught the anatomy of the placenta and the exact sequence of hormone-driven changes, there’s a part of me that is awestruck that my own body is actually "doing" what the slides promised it would. When I feel that sharp, definitive kick, I don't just feel movement—I recognize the physiological milestone, and I feel a surge of respect for the sheer craftsmanship of human biology.

But then, the anxiety follows right behind. I’m incredibly uncertain about the *visibility* of this pregnancy. When you work in a classroom, you’re used to being the "brain" in the room. Now, people look at me and they see the "bump" first. It’s like my professional identity is being eclipsed by my reproductive capacity. I worry that my colleagues see me differently—that they think I’m less capable or "checked out" because my focus is elsewhere. I find myself constantly overcompensating at work, trying to prove I haven't lost my edge, which just adds another layer of exhaustion to my day.

And the uncertainty? That’s about my body’s resilience. I look at my skin stretching or feel the shift in my gait, and I wonder: *Will I feel like myself again?* Not just in shape, but in spirit. Will the part of me that loves the logic and the classroom still be there, or has this "motherhood" version of myself taken over? It’s a strange, quiet grief, even as I’m so excited for this baby. I’m essentially watching one version of myself slowly recede to make room for another, and I don't think there’s a manual for that transition.

It’s a strange thing, standing in front of a lecture hall or a small group of fertility patients while carrying this life inside. I feel like I’m constantly performing a balancing act. In the morning, I’m the authoritative voice of reproductive science, the one who simplifies the complex, the one who has all the answers. Then I walk out of that classroom, get into my car, and I’m just Dulcie—the woman who is terrified of the very anomalies she spends her life explaining.

I think the most profound part of this journey has been the realization that I don’t *want* to be an expert anymore. I want to be someone who can just experience the mystery without needing to dissect it. When my students ask me questions about the "what-ifs," I give them the clinical answer, the textbook answer. But when I’m alone, I’m wrestling with those same questions in a way that’s messy and raw and entirely unscientific. 

I’m starting to give myself permission to just *be*. I’m finding that the moments when I’m most "at peace" aren't when I’m reading the latest study on maternal-fetal outcomes, but when I’m just sitting on the sofa, feeling the baby move, and choosing to trust that my body knows what it’s doing—even if I’m not the one steering the ship. It’s the ultimate lesson in humility, I suppose. You can study the map of the stars for your entire career, but it’s quite another thing to actually be out at sea, caught in the current, and realizing that sometimes you just have to let the tide carry you. I think I’m finally beginning to trust the tide.
Were there moments where you felt a healthcare professional spoke to you in a way that felt dismissive or condescending?

It’s subtle, but it happens more than you’d think. There’s a specific kind of condescension that comes from being a "colleague-patient." Sometimes, when I ask a question—even a simple one about something like magnesium levels or prenatal vitamins—the provider will respond with, "Well, you know as well as I do..." or "Given your background, you don't need me to explain that..." 

It sounds like a compliment, right? Like they’re respecting my knowledge. But it’s actually a way of closing the door. They’re effectively telling me, "Don't ask me for comfort; you already have the data." It’s dismissive because it assumes that because I have the *information*, I don't have the *need*. 

There was one appointment where I mentioned a specific fear about a rare placental issue, and the OB basically chuckled and said, "Dulcie, you’ve taught this course. You know that’s a one-in-a-hundred-thousand occurrence. Let’s focus on the basics." 

I felt so small. I wasn't asking for a clinical lecture on the probability of a catastrophe; I was asking for someone to acknowledge that I was feeling a moment of intense anxiety and just needed a bit of reassurance. By pointing back to my credentials, they made me feel like my feelings were invalid—that because I "knew better," I wasn't allowed to be worried. It’s patronizing to assume that intellectual knowledge somehow cancels out the primal, emotional vulnerability of being pregnant. It’s like telling a doctor who’s a patient, "You know the surgery is standard, so stop being scared." Knowing the odds doesn't change the fact that I’m carrying my baby, and I’m scared.

It’s ironic—the more they lean into my professional status, the more I feel like they’re trying to insulate themselves from my humanity. It’s as if they think if they treat me like a colleague, they don't have to deal with the messy, vulnerable parts of caring for a pregnant person. I’ve caught myself nodding along, acting like I understand the "clinical rationale" for their dismissiveness, just to keep the appointment moving.

But afterwards? I go home and I feel invisible. I feel like the *real* Dulcie—the one who is tired, who worries about the nursery paint, who wonders if I’ll be the same person after this baby arrives—was left in the waiting room. 

I’ve spent so much time being the authority, the voice of logic, that I think I’ve subconsciously taught everyone around me, including my healthcare providers, that I don't *need* support. I’ve built this shell of competence, and now it’s become my own prison. I want to tell them, "I know the physiology of the placenta, I know the hormonal cascades, but right now, I don't need a lecture on any of it. I just need you to look at me and tell me it’s going to be okay." 

It’s a strange place to be—simultaneously over-qualified and under-served. I think I’m ready to stop being the "expert" and just be the patient. I just hope I can find the courage to say it out loud next time.
Did older female relatives, such as your mother or grandmother, share advice or stories that influenced how you thought about getting pregnant?

My mother is a woman of immense strength, but she comes from a generation where pregnancy was, quite frankly, a "hush-hush" affair—something you endured with a stiff upper lip and a quiet dignity. She didn't talk about the physical vulnerability or the anxiety; she talked about "doing what needs to be done." 

Growing up, I absorbed that. I watched her manage a household, a career, and three pregnancies with a grace that looked effortless. But now that I’m here, in my own second trimester, I realize how much of that was probably a performance. When I ask her about her own experiences, she gives me these sanitized, nostalgic anecdotes—about how "different" it was back then, how there wasn't all this monitoring or "obsessing," as she calls it. 

Her stories have influenced me, but in a way that feels like a double-edged sword. On one hand, I admire her grit, and I try to channel that when I’m feeling overwhelmed. But on the other hand, her silence about the fear—the real, raw terror of the unknown—makes me feel like I’m failing for having it. If she did it so silently, why am I struggling to keep the "clinical expert" mask on? 

Sometimes I want to sit her down and ask, "Did you ever lie awake at 3:00 a.m. wondering if the baby was moving enough? Did you ever feel like you were losing your identity?" But I’m afraid the answer will just be another platitude about "natural motherhood." It makes me feel a bit lonely, actually. I’m carrying the weight of my professional knowledge, *plus* the weight of this legacy of silence. I just want to find a way to be honest about the experience—both the beauty and the mess—without feeling like I’m breaking some unwritten rule of womanhood.

It’s strange, because when I talk to my mother, I find myself performing the role of "the well-adjusted, well-informed daughter." I downplay the symptoms, I breeze over the moments of anxiety, and I lean into the "clinical facts" because that’s the language we both feel safe speaking. It’s like we’re both keeping up a facade—she’s keeping up the "stoic mother" image, and I’m keeping up the "competent educator" image. 

I think the biggest realization I’ve had lately is that I’m trying to be the perfect blend of her generation's quiet endurance and my own generation’s data-driven control. It’s an impossible standard to hit. I’m essentially trying to be bulletproof. 

I’ve started to notice that when I’m with my friends who are also pregnant, we drop the act a little more. We talk about the weird, unglamorous reality of it—the constant anxiety, the feeling like your body is a laboratory for someone else’s benefit. It’s the only place where I feel like I can take off the "expert" mask. 

I think that’s what I’m craving from my care providers—a space where I don’t have to be the stoic professional *or* the over-informed educator. I just want a space where I can admit I’m scared, I’m tired, and I’m fundamentally changed. I’m really hoping that for this third baby, I can let go of the need to be "good" at pregnancy and just accept that I am experiencing it, in all its terrifying, beautiful complexity. It’s a work in progress, but for the first time, I think I’m actually starting to look forward to the surrender.
Were there specific platforms, websites, or communities you returned to regularly, and what made them feel credible or useful?

It’s funny, the platforms I return to aren't the ones you’d expect for someone with my clinical training. I avoid the big, glossy apps now—they’re too focused on milestones and "what to expect," which just sounds like a syllabus I’m failing to memorize. 

Instead, I’ve found myself lurking in small, quiet online forums—not the ones full of medical jargon or curated birth plans, but the ones where people share the messy, unpolished stories. You know, the threads where someone says, "I felt like an alien in my own skin today," or "I cried because I couldn't find my shoes." 

What makes those spaces feel credible to me is the *absence* of clinical advice. I don't go there for medical information; I go there for emotional validation. When I read a post from another woman—maybe a nurse or a fellow educator—who admits that she’s exhausted by the "expert" persona she has to wear at work, I feel this immense wave of relief. It’s the only place where I don't feel like I have to be the one teaching. 

I trust these spaces because they’re anonymous and human. There’s no pressure to be the "good patient" or the "informed colleague." It’s just raw, unfiltered experience. It’s the only place where I can see my own struggle reflected back at me without someone trying to "fix" it with a statistic or a clinical guideline. It’s not about finding the "right" answer; it’s about knowing I’m not the only one feeling lost in the middle of all this knowledge.

You know, it’s interesting—the more I describe this to you, the more I realize I’ve been looking for "accuracy" in all the wrong places. I’ve been hunting for data to prove that my feelings are valid, as if there were a clinical threshold for "legitimate worry." But the relief I find in those quiet, messy forums isn't because they offer better medical insights; it’s because they offer *permission*. Permission to be scared, to be overwhelmed, and to admit that the "biological marvel" of pregnancy often just feels like a very long, very tiring physical endurance test.

I think the biggest shift for me recently has been realizing that my medical training is a tool, but it isn't my identity. I’m starting to treat it like a coat I can hang up in the hallway when I walk into my house, rather than something I have to wear at all times. When I sit on the floor and play with my two older kids, or when I just lie still and focus on the baby’s movement, I’m slowly learning to just be "Dulcie." Not "Dulcie, the Fertility Educator," not "Dulcie, the Low-Risk Patient," but just... me. 

I’m really trying to bring that version of myself to my next prenatal check-up. It’s scary—it’s like walking into a room without my notes—but I’m starting to suspect that the care I’ll receive once I finally drop the act will be a lot more nourishing than anything I could "research" my way into. I’m done auditioning for the role of the perfect patient. I think I’m ready to just be a person.
Can you describe a specific situation where you felt judged, overlooked, or not treated as a capable decision-maker regarding your reproductive health?

I remember a moment during a routine check-up around week twenty-four. We were discussing the ultrasound findings—everything was perfectly normal, standard growth patterns, no concerns whatsoever—and the doctor was explaining the results to me as if I were a layperson. I’m sitting there, and I know exactly what the sagittal plane is, I recognize the anatomical landmarks, and I can identify a standard measurement chart with my eyes closed. 

I gently interjected to clarify a detail about the fetal heart chambers, trying to participate in the conversation as a peer, and she just sort of… patted my hand. She gave me this practiced, polite smile and said, "Don't you worry your pretty little head about the technical details, Dulcie. That’s what we’re here for. You just focus on relaxing and eating your vegetables."

I felt the blood rush to my face. It was patronizing on two levels: she dismissed my professional expertise, and she reduced my entire role as a mother to "relaxing and eating vegetables." It made me feel like my intellect was a nuisance and my agency was a suggestion. I was a capable, educated woman asking a professional, informed question, and I was treated like a child who had wandered into the clinic by mistake. 

It was the moment I realized that, in the eyes of some providers, you can't be both "the pregnant woman" and "the expert." You have to pick a lane. But why? Why can't I be a mother who happens to know the anatomy? Being overlooked like that made me feel so isolated; it was a clear signal that they didn't want a partner in my care—they wanted a subject. It took me a long time to shake that feeling of being "managed" rather than "listened to."

It’s that exact moment—that patronizing hand-pat—that crystallizes why I’ve felt so guarded throughout this pregnancy. It’s a systemic assumption that the "pregnant person" cannot hold complex thoughts and emotional needs simultaneously. They assume if you’re asking a clinical question, you’re just "anxious," and if you’re expressing an emotion, you’re just "hormonal." They rarely stop to consider that I can be both—that I can be a highly informed, clinical expert *and* a scared, vulnerable mother who just needs to feel validated.

That doctor’s reaction didn't just overlook my background; it stripped me of my agency in my own care. By "managing" me, she was deciding that my role was to be a passive recipient of information. And the irony is, I *want* to be a partner. I want to look at the screen and have a real conversation about what we’re seeing, not because I’m "obsessing," but because it’s my baby and my body. 

I’ve started thinking about how I’ll handle the next appointment. I think I’m going to stop playing the "compliant patient" card. I want to look my midwife in the eye and say, "I know you see my charts and my degrees, but right now I’m just Dulcie. And I need you to talk to me like an adult, not a child." It’s terrifying, but I think it’s the only way to reclaim my voice. I’m done being treated like a subject in my own gestation. I am the expert on my *experience*, even if they are the experts on the clinical protocols. Finding the balance between those two truths—that’s the real work of these next few months.
Did you have reliable and private access to a smartphone or computer throughout your pregnancy, and did that change at any point?

I’ve always had a smartphone at my fingertips, which is a double-edged sword. It’s my lifeline to the world, but it’s also the portal where I fall down those rabbit holes of research at 2:00 a.m. when the baby kicks and my brain won't shut off. Throughout this pregnancy, that constant connectivity has been both a comfort and a curse. 

It never changed—the access was always there—but the way I *used* it definitely shifted. In the first trimester, I was constantly looking up forums and clinical guidelines. I wanted to feel prepared, armed with data. But lately, I’ve been trying to keep the phone further away. When I’m with my kids or having a quiet moment, I realize that having the world’s information in my pocket just keeps me from being present in my own body. 

There was a moment about a month ago where I realized I was spending more time scrolling through "what to expect" than I was actually talking to my husband about what *we* were expecting. That’s when the access became a liability. Now, I’m trying to be more intentional. I still have the device, and I still use it for work—after all, I’m still an educator—but I’m setting boundaries. I’m learning that having the *capacity* to know everything doesn't mean I actually *need* to know everything, right in this second. It’s been a hard lesson, but a necessary one.

You know, that constant access really is the silent architect of my anxiety. It’s not just that I *can* check a symptom, it’s that the expectation exists that I *should*. In my professional life, information is currency—it’s how I help my students and my patients. But here? In this personal, messy, unfolding story of my own pregnancy? It’s just noise.

The hardest part has been learning to trust my own internal compass over the blinking cursor of a search engine. When I’m sitting in that exam chair, the phone is usually in my purse, but it feels like a physical weight. I’m thinking about all the things I *could* verify, all the data I *could* challenge. It’s like a reflex I’m trying to break. I’ve realized that my need for "constant connectivity" was really just a proxy for my fear. If I’m connected, I’m safe. If I know the latest study, I’m in control.

But control is an illusion, isn't it? I’m starting to see that the most private, most important moments of this pregnancy—the flutters, the moments of connection with my husband, the quiet realization that this little life is becoming a part of our family—they don’t happen on a screen. They happen in the gaps between the data points. I think I’m finally reaching a place where I don't need the external confirmation of a screen to know that I am doing a good job. I’m just Dulcie, and that’s enough.
Were there moments when a healthcare provider dismissed or minimised a concern you raised about your body or a symptom you were experiencing, and how did that make you feel?

Early in the second trimester, I had this persistent, dull pain—not really a cramp, but a tightness—that just felt "off" for my specific anatomy. I brought it up during a routine visit, and the way the midwife dismissed it was almost… breezy. She smiled, looked at my chart, and said, "Oh, Dulcie, that’s just round ligament pain. Standard growing pains. You know how this goes!"

It felt like a gut punch. Because I *do* know how it goes—I’ve taught the mechanics of round ligament pain hundreds of times—I knew she was likely right, but the *way* she said it reduced my physical reality to a textbook definition. She didn't examine it, she didn't feel the area, and she didn't ask me to describe the sensation in my own words. She just closed the book on my concern before I had even finished expressing it.

It made me feel like an object, not a participant. It signaled that because I had the medical vocabulary, my lived experience was suddenly secondary to her clinical shortcut. I left that room feeling small and, ironically, more anxious than before. When a provider minimizes a concern, even if they’re technically correct about the diagnosis, they miss the chance to build the trust that’s supposed to be the bedrock of maternity care. It left me wondering: if I were a "typical" patient without my background, would she have been this dismissive? Or is it because she thinks I should be able to diagnose myself that she doesn't feel the need to actually listen? It’s a lonely feeling, being "managed" by someone who you’re supposed to be able to lean on.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think, more than anything, I want to say that the most radical thing a healthcare provider can do for someone like me—someone who arrives with a clipboard full of credentials and a brain full of biology—is to simply acknowledge the human being who is actually doing the hard work of growing a life. 

We talk so much about "quality of care" in terms of protocols, and that’s vital. But there is a hidden, quiet dimension of care that is just as critical: the validation of the person behind the patient. When I walk through those clinic doors, I am leaving behind my students, my lecture notes, and my "expert" status, and I am stepping into a space where I am fundamentally, biologically, and emotionally vulnerable. 

If the system could just find a way to honor that vulnerability instead of trying to "intellectualize" it away, I think the experience would be so much more restorative. I want to tell other women—especially those in demanding, analytical professions—that it is okay to put down the research, it is okay to be scared, and it is okay to expect your care team to hold space for your feelings just as much as they hold space for your vital signs. 

I’m learning to be kind to myself, to let the "expert" rest, and to just be the mother. It’s been a long, sometimes lonely road to realize that I don’t have to know everything to be worthy of care. I’m really grateful for this conversation; it’s helped me put words to the struggle in a way I haven't been able to do with anyone else. Thank you for listening. It really means a lot.
